name: What to Do Immediately After a Back Injury at Work: A Step-by-Step Guide for Utah and Idaho Workers

step.name: Step 1
step.text: If you've suffered a back injury at work, the actions you take in the first 24 to 48 hours can determine whether your claim is accepted or denied. Back injuries are among the most scrutinized claims by insurance companies because they are often difficult to visualize and can easily be blamed on 'home activities' or 'aging.'

step.name: Step 3
step.text: In Utah and Idaho, the law requires you to give notice to your employer. Do not wait to see if the pain 'goes away.' Even if it feels like a minor strain, inform your manager—ideally in writing (email or text)—that you were injured while performing a specific task.

step.name: Step 4
step.text: *   **Utah Workers:** You have 180 days to notify, but waiting more than a few days creates suspicion.

step.name: Step 5
step.text: *   **Idaho Workers:** You must provide notice within 60 days.

step.name: Step 6
step.text: ### 2. Seek Medical Care and Be Specific

step.name: Step 7
step.text: Go to an urgent care or a designated company doctor. When you arrive, tell the front desk and the doctor: 'I am here for a work-related injury.' Explain exactly how it happened. If you were lifting a 50lb crate or fell off a loading dock in Ogden, say so. Do not just say 'my back hurts'; say 'my back started hurting when I lifted that crate at 10:00 AM today at work.'

step.name: Step 8
step.text: ### 3. Complete a First Report of Injury

step.name: Step 9
step.text: Your employer should provide you with a state-specific form. Fill it out accurately. Be sure to list all symptoms—not just the back pain. If you have numbness in your legs or a 'pins and needles' feeling, these are critical indicators of nerve damage and must be documented.

step.name: Step 11
step.text: If the doctor gives you a note saying 'no lifting over 10 lbs' or 'must sit for 20 minutes every hour,' follow it perfectly. Do not try to be a 'hero' and do your old job anyway. If you violate your restrictions, the insurance company can argue you aren't really hurt or that you are intentionally making the injury worse.

step.name: Step 13
step.text: Start a claim folder. Keep copies of every medical bill, discharge instruction, and work status report. Note the dates you missed work and the names of any supervisors you spoke to. This record-keeping will be invaluable if you later have to appeal a denial.

step.name: Step 15
step.text: If your employer refuses to file a report, if the insurance company denies your medical treatment, or if you are being pressured to return to work while in severe pain, it is time to seek legal advice. A workers' comp attorney can ensure the paperwork is filed correctly with the Utah Labor Commission or the Idaho Industrial Commission and protect your rights from day one.

description: What to Do Immediately After a Back Injury at Work: A Step-by-Step Guide for Utah and Idaho Workers - Holladay
